segments.text: how's it going you guys it's scott with everyday home repairs and today we'll actually be repairing a tire this is on my trailer but the same process is valid if your vehicle had a screw like this one or a nail through the treads we're not going to be messing with anything through the sidewall but in this case if you're through the treads like this it's a pretty approachable project to fix it very inexpensively and you can save yourself a few dollars and get yourself out of a tough situation so let's jump into it

Now I'm very confident that this is a source of a leak. My tire's starting to go flat on this trailer, but if you need some help, you can actually just mix some water and dish soap or really any kind of soap.

Pour that water over your suspected leak point, and you should see bubbles like this forming that would confirm that we have a slow leak at the screw or nail. I'm just using a simple kit. It can cost between $6 to $10. It's made by Slime. We have our puncture tool, which is gonna help us puncture the plug through the tire. We have a reamer tool, which is gonna help clean out and get any debris out of the actual hole.

it comes with some additional plug bond which helps the actual plug set up and then it'll come with several different plugs which are going to actually be what stops this leak now if you need a reference below the video you'll see a link to our amazon store and in the general supply section you'll see this exact kit that i'm using it's nice to just get it and have it in your car because again this could get you out of a situation where you're kind of stranded on the roadside where you can stop a leak and then limp over to a station and actually pull

put some air in your tire. Also, I'll show you a little bit later on how I keep these plugs from drying out so I can store them in my truck. So first up, what I'm gonna do is I'll put the plug through the actual puncture tool. I want this to be ready. So when I start to remove the screw, I ream out the hole there with this rasp.

that I'm able then just to insert this. So you pull that plug where it's equal sides across this tool, and then you're ready. Now, if you want to, you can also use this plug bond and put a little bit on the plug itself, which should help it set up once it's in the tool on our last step.

Then you'll set this off to the side. Make sure you keep it on a clean surface because you don't want to get any dirt or debris on it while you're getting the screw out or nail out and cleaning up that puncture. So usually you can just use a set of some type of pliers. These are actually hybrid wire strippers, and I'll actually be able to use the threads on this screw to help back it out.

So you can start to hear the air will start leaking out. Then you can kind of cover that up while you get your tool to clean that out ready. And you want to work that in and out. Again, getting any debris out of that and then also cleaning up that hole. If you need to grab your plug, you can just leave that tool in. Then you'll get your plug ready. Go ahead and remove that tool.
and then firmly press in that plug and then once that plugs set in there all you have to do is quickly remove this insertion tool

Now, one key also is we did remove a nail or screw, right? So make sure you put that in your pocket and not just back on the driveway so we don't just repuncture our tire or someone else's tire. And I'll just go ahead and wipe off that extra cement. And then you can use just with the razor blade, you'll just go ahead and take off any extra plug.

from the tire. And there's a little bit closer look at the plug in the tread. Looks good. Then it's not a bad idea to go ahead and keep both of those tools in your truck. I put it in my toolbox and then I use a one inch PVC just with a few caps. And then this is big enough to actually hold the smaller heavy duty plugs. And then your longer plugs, you can fit those guys in there. They can go all the way down and then you can even

just go ahead and cap that off all this is is a one inch pvc about five and a half or six inches long and then just two simple caps and then that will hopefully help those from drying out long term so then when you need them next maybe on the side of a road you're ready to go with your tools and also your plugs that aren't dried out and then we'll do a final test with the soapy water to make sure we're not getting any bubbles anymore and we have our leak fixed so just use that same cup

And we can see there's no bubbles forming now. So this plug should be good to go and we will no longer have a leak on this tire. So now I can get back to work with this trailer and not worry about this tire going flat. Now, if you want to check out a cool project I did last summer where I actually used a pool noodle to help seal off a large crack in a sidewalk. Believe it or not, it's not a gimmick. It actually works awesome. And that project is still holding up strong one year later. So thanks for joining me on this video and we'll catch you on the next one. Take care.
